attrs.alt: B2B Invitation Flow and Permission Controls

content.text: Inviting external users is the primary way to enable B2B collaboration in Microsoft Entra ID. An invitation creates a pending guest object that is confirmed when the recipient redeems the link. You can invite users via the Azure Portal, PowerShell, Graph API, CSV bulk upload, or indirectly through SharePoint/Teams sharing.
Invitations are not permanent — they expire after 30 days by default. A guest who does not redeem within this window must be re-invited.
Invitation permissions are controlled by the External Collaboration Settings policy. The four levels from most permissive to most restrictive are:
Global Admins + Guest Inviter role holders

The simplest invitation path is the Azure Portal: Entra ID → Users → New user → Invite external user. For scripting and automation, New-MgInvitation in the Microsoft.Graph.Identity.SignIns module provides equivalent capability.

# Portal path: Azure Portal → Microsoft Entra ID → Users → New user → Invite external user
# Then set: Email address, Display name, Message (optional), Groups/Roles (optional)

# Equivalent PowerShell using Microsoft Graph SDK
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'User.Invite.All'

New-MgInvitation `
    -InvitedUserEmailAddress 'partner@fabrikam.com' `
    -InvitedUserDisplayName 'Alex Fabrikam' `
    -InviteRedirectUrl 'https://myapps.microsoft.com' `
    -SendInvitationMessage:$true `
    -InvitedUserMessageInfo @{
        messageLanguage = 'en-US'
        customizedMessageBody = 'Welcome to the Contoso partner portal. Please sign in to access shared resources.'
    }

When onboarding many partners at once, use a CSV file listing email addresses and display names. The loop calls New-MgInvitation for each row and exports a results file containing the redemption URL for each invited user.

# guest-list.csv content:
# emailAddress,displayName,redirectUrl
# partner1@fabrikam.com,Alex Fabrikam,https://myapps.microsoft.com
# user2@contoso.com,Bob Contoso,https://myapps.microsoft.com

# Bulk invite from CSV
$invites = Import-Csv -Path ./guest-list.csv
foreach ($inv in $invites) {
    $result = New-MgInvitation `
        -InvitedUserEmailAddress $inv.emailAddress `
        -InvitedUserDisplayName $inv.displayName `
        -InviteRedirectUrl $inv.redirectUrl `
        -SendInvitationMessage:$true
    [pscustomobject]@{
        Email   = $inv.emailAddress
        Status  = $result.status
        RedeemUrl = $result.inviteRedeemUrl
    }
} | Export-Csv -Path ./invite-results.csv -NoTypeInformation
Write-Host 'Bulk invite complete. Results saved to invite-results.csv'

The POST /v1.0/invitations endpoint supports custom portals, external onboarding systems, and headless automation. The User.Invite.All permission is required — as a delegated permission the caller's role still governs who they can invite.

# POST https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/invitations
# Requires: User.Invite.All (delegated or app permission)

$headers = @{
    Authorization = "Bearer $accessToken"
    'Content-Type' = 'application/json'
}

$body = @{
    invitedUserEmailAddress = 'partner@fabrikam.com'
    invitedUserDisplayName  = 'Alex Fabrikam'
    inviteRedirectUrl       = 'https://myapps.microsoft.com'
    sendInvitationMessage   = $true
    invitedUserType         = 'Guest'
    invitedUserMessageInfo  = @{
        messageLanguage = 'en-US'
        customizedMessageBody = 'Welcome to Contoso collaboration.'
    }
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 3

$resp = Invoke-RestMethod -Method POST \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/invitations' \
    -Headers $headers -Body $body

# Response includes: id, inviteRedeemUrl, invitedUser.id, status
Write-Host "Invite status: $($resp.status)"
Write-Host "Redemption URL: $($resp.inviteRedeemUrl)"
Write-Host "Guest object ID: $($resp.invitedUser.id)"

Lock down which external email domains are allowed for invitations using the authorization policy. You can operate an allowlist (only invited domains pass), a blocklist (all domains except listed ones pass), or combine them. Leave both empty to allow all domains.

# Configure invitation domain restrictions
# Allowlist: only allow invitations to specific domains
$settings = Get-MgPolicyAuthorizationPolicy
$currentPolicy = $settings | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5 | ConvertFrom-Json

# Set allowed domains (blocks all other domains)
$policyBody = @{
    allowedToInviteExternalUsers = $true
    allowInvitesFrom = 'adminsAndGuestInviters'
    allowedExternalUserEmailDomains = @('fabrikam.com', 'partner.org', 'vendor.net')
} | ConvertTo-Json

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authorizationPolicy' \
    -Body $policyBody -ContentType 'application/json'

# Alternatively, use a blocklist to deny specific domains
$blockBody = @{
    allowedExternalUserEmailDomains = @()   # empty = no allowlist
    blockedExternalUserEmailDomains = @('competitor.com', 'risky-domain.net')
} | ConvertTo-Json

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PATCH \
    -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authorizationPolicy' \
    -Body $blockBody -ContentType 'application/json'

With SAML/WS-Fed direct federation configured for a partner domain, you can skip email invitations entirely. When a user from the federated domain navigates to your application, Entra ID redirects them to their home IdP, authenticates them, and provisions the guest object automatically on first sign-in.

# Just-In-Time (JIT) invitation via SAML response
# When a user from a federated domain signs in for the first time,
# Entra ID can auto-create the guest object without a prior invitation.

# Enable JIT provisioning for a SAML/WS-Fed partner
$jitBody = @{
    '@odata.type' = '#microsoft.graph.samlOrWsFedExternalDomainFederation'
    issuerUri = 'https://sts.fabrikam.com/adfs/services/trust'
    metadataExchangeUri = 'https://sts.fabrikam.com/adfs/services/trust/mex'
    signingCertificate = '<base64-cert>'
    passiveSignInUri = 'https://sts.fabrikam.com/adfs/ls/'
    preferredAuthenticationProtocol = 'saml'
    domains = @( @{ id = 'fabrikam.com' } )
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 4

# After federation is configured, users navigating directly to your app
# are redirected to fabrikam.com ADFS for authentication.
# On successful auth, Entra ID provisions the guest object automatically.
# No email invitation is required.

Audit Logs capture every invitation sent and redeemed. Use the query below to surface unredeemed invitations older than 14 days — these represent open access tokens that should be cancelled and re-issued if still needed.

// Track invitation redemptions and pending invites
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where OperationName in (
    'Invite external user',
    'Redeem external user invite'
)
| extend
    GuestEmail = tostring(TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName),
    InvitedBy  = tostring(InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName),
    EventType  = OperationName
| project TimeGenerated, EventType, GuestEmail, InvitedBy
| order by TimeGenerated desc

// Find invitations sent but never redeemed (>14 days old)
let Invited = AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(60d)
| where OperationName == 'Invite external user'
| extend GuestEmail = tostring(TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName), InvitedAt = TimeGenerated;
let Redeemed = AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(60d)
| where OperationName == 'Redeem external user invite'
| extend GuestEmail = tostring(TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName);
Invited
| join kind=leftanti Redeemed on GuestEmail
| where InvitedAt < ago(14d)
| project GuestEmail, InvitedAt
| order by InvitedAt asc
Use -ResetRedemption:$true to generate a new invitation link for a guest whose previous link expired or whose email address changed. This invalidates any prior redemption URL.

# Resend invitation to a guest whose link expired or who never redeemed
$guestUPN = 'partner_fabrikam.com#EXT#@contoso.onmicrosoft.com'
$guest = Get-MgUser -Filter "userPrincipalName eq '$guestUPN'"

if ($guest.ExternalUserState -eq 'PendingAcceptance') {
    # Get the original email address from the identities collection
    $emailIdentity = $guest.Identities | Where-Object { $_.SignInType -eq 'emailAddress' }
    $email = $emailIdentity.IssuerAssignedId

    # Re-invite
    New-MgInvitation `
        -InvitedUserEmailAddress $email `
        -InviteRedirectUrl 'https://myapps.microsoft.com' `
        -SendInvitationMessage:$true `
        -ResetRedemption:$true
    Write-Host "Re-invited: $email"
} else {
    Write-Host "Guest $guestUPN has already redeemed (state: $($guest.ExternalUserState))"
}
Fallback auth for users with no federated IdP
Allows external users to request access without admin
Restricts invitation targets by email domain
Whether guests can themselves invite other guests
Cannot invite users from this domain
Check for soft-deleted user in audit logs; wait 30 days or hard-delete
Share the inviteRedeemUrl directly instead of email
Guest object created but not assigned to app/group
Assign the guest to the required app or group explicitly

Invite-and-assign is a common pattern: send the invitation, capture the guest object ID from the response, and immediately assign the guest to an application role or resource. This ensures the guest can access the correct resources as soon as they redeem.

# Assign an app role to a guest immediately after invitation
# Step 1: invite
$invite = New-MgInvitation `
    -InvitedUserEmailAddress 'partner@fabrikam.com' `
    -InviteRedirectUrl 'https://myapps.microsoft.com' `
    -SendInvitationMessage:$true

$guestId = $invite.InvitedUser.Id

# Step 2: find the enterprise application and app role
$appId = 'aaaabbbb-1234-5678-9abc-def012345678'   # your app's object ID
$app = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "appId eq '$appId'"
$role = $app.AppRoles | Where-Object { $_.DisplayName -eq 'Partner.Read' }

# Step 3: assign the role to the guest
New-MgUserAppRoleAssignment `
    -UserId $guestId `
    -PrincipalId $guestId `
    -ResourceId $app.Id `
    -AppRoleId $role.Id
Write-Host "Guest $($invite.InvitedUserEmailAddress) assigned role: $($role.DisplayName)"

Security group membership is the most common way to grant guests access to SharePoint sites, Teams channels, and conditional application policies. Add the guest to the group in the same script run that sends the invitation.

# Add a guest to a security group after invitation (for resource access)
$invite = New-MgInvitation `
    -InvitedUserEmailAddress 'vendor@supplier.com' `
    -InviteRedirectUrl 'https://myapps.microsoft.com' `
    -SendInvitationMessage:$true

$guestId = $invite.InvitedUser.Id

# Add to an existing security group
$groupId = 'ccccdddd-5678-9012-bcde-f01234567890'
New-MgGroupMember -GroupId $groupId -DirectoryObjectId $guestId
Write-Host "Guest added to group: $groupId"

# Verify group membership
Get-MgGroupMember -GroupId $groupId |
    Where-Object { $_.Id -eq $guestId } |
    ForEach-Object { Write-Host 'Group membership confirmed' }

When a member shares a SharePoint site, folder, or file with an external email address, SharePoint automatically triggers a B2B invitation if no guest object exists for that email. Use PnP PowerShell to manage external sharing programmatically.

# Sharing a SharePoint site with an external user triggers B2B invitation
# When a member shares a file/folder/site with an external email,
# SharePoint automatically sends a B2B invitation if one hasn't been sent yet.

# Using PnP PowerShell to share a site with an external user
Connect-PnPOnline -Url 'https://contoso.sharepoint.com/sites/PartnerPortal' `
    -Interactive

# Add external user as site collection administrator (use sparingly)
Add-PnPSiteCollectionAdmin -Owners 'partner@fabrikam.com'

# Or add as a regular site member (safer)
Add-PnPUser -LoginName 'partner@fabrikam.com'
Set-PnPRoleDefinitionPermission -Identity 'partner@fabrikam.com' -Role 'Read'

# The external user receives a B2B invitation email and must redeem before accessing
The azuread Terraform provider exposes an azuread_invitation resource. This lets you manage B2B invitations alongside other Azure infrastructure in version-controlled Terraform state.

Track what percentage of invitations are actually redeemed. A low redemption rate often indicates delivery issues (spam filters), incorrect email addresses, or user confusion. Week-over-week trends help identify systemic problems.

// Count guests by redemption state
// Requires Microsoft Entra ID audit logs in Log Analytics
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(90d)
| where OperationName == 'Invite external user'
| extend
    GuestEmail = tostring(TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName),
    InvitedAt  = TimeGenerated,
    InvitedBy  = tostring(InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName)
| join kind=leftouter (
    AuditLogs
    | where OperationName == 'Redeem external user invite'
    | extend GuestEmail = tostring(TargetResources[0].userPrincipalName),
             RedeemedAt = TimeGenerated
) on GuestEmail
| extend State = iff(isnotempty(RedeemedAt), 'Redeemed', 'Pending')
| summarize
    TotalInvited  = count(),
    Redeemed      = countif(State == 'Redeemed'),
    Pending       = countif(State == 'Pending'),
    RedemptionRate = round(100.0 * countif(State == 'Redeemed') / count(), 1)
    by bin(InvitedAt, 7d)
| order by InvitedAt desc
Invitations can be sent via portal, PowerShell, Graph API, CSV bulk upload, or JIT via SAML federation.
The External Collaboration Settings policy controls who in your tenant can send invitations.
Domain restrictions (allow/blocklist) limit which external email domains can be invited.
Invitation links expire in 30 days by default; re-invite with -ResetRedemption:$true to refresh.
Use KQL against Audit Logs to track unredeemed invitations and alert on stale open invites.
JIT provisioning via SAML/WS-Fed direct federation eliminates the email invitation step for large partner organizations.
